caveats: a: The occupation table is May 2023 and reports employment categories rather than task content.
a: Google DORA surveyed technology professionals globally, not only U.S. lower-middle-market software publishers.
a: The injected labor-intensity value is inherited from ancestor sector 51 and is LOW quality, so it may misstate this industry's compensation-to-receipts structure.
rho: BTOS covers the entire Information sector rather than NAICS 513210 alone.
rho: Reported tool use and perceived productivity are not equivalent to durable labor-cost realization.
rho: DORA's trust findings imply review work can offset some gross time savings.
e: The NAICS definition establishes activity, not profitability, independence, size, or revenue recurrence.
e: The pricing survey covers 100 SaaS companies and is not a representative census of all software publishers.
e: The firm-count input n is a declared dataset gap and must remain MISSING; this estimate does not replace or infer n.
s5: Kroll's figures are global and cover the broader software sector across transaction sizes.
s5: Deal counts do not identify unique sellers or provide the denominator needed for a seller-incidence rate.
s5: Financing conditions and software valuation cycles can make a single period unrepresentative of a full five-year window.
q: Pricing architecture indicates potential value capture but does not directly measure retained labor savings.
q: The survey is small and focused on SaaS, excluding important software-publisher business models.
q: AI may simultaneously lower costs and increase competitive entry or infrastructure spending.
d5: Employment growth is only a proxy for constant-price demand and spans occupations across many industries.
d5: Product mix varies from mature maintenance software to rapidly growing AI-native applications.
d5: Nominal subscription growth can reflect price changes rather than real service demand.
o: Persistence differs sharply between system-of-record software and lightly differentiated point applications.
o: The estimate concerns the need for an operating entity, not whether current staffing levels persist.
o: AI may create new publisher categories while displacing existing products.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Businesses and consumers pay recurring subscriptions, usage-based charges, license fees, maintenance, support, advertising, or transaction-linked fees for standardized software products. Revenue can be highly recurring, although consumer applications, games, perpetual licenses, and usage-sensitive products are less predictable.
lens.excluded_activities: Custom software development performed primarily for one client, captive internal software teams, pure IT consulting, infrastructure hosting without a published software product, and pre-revenue projects without a demonstrated external customer model.
lens.included_activities: Commercial software publishers serving external customers through subscriptions, term or perpetual licenses, usage charges, maintenance, support, and related recurring services, viewed at lower-middle-market EBITDA scale.

a.bridge: BLS occupation shares for the Software Publishers industry are converted into a wage-weighted judgment of five-year AI-addressable labor tasks. The estimate assigns substantial exposure to computer, business, management, sales, and administrative work while preserving human shares for architecture, security, customer discovery, governance, and accountability. Occupations are not tasks, so the bridge may overstate automation where judgment dominates and understate exposure within nominally nontechnical roles.
caveats: The occupation table is May 2023 and reports employment categories rather than task content.
Google DORA surveyed technology professionals globally, not only U.S. lower-middle-market software publishers.
The injected labor-intensity value is inherited from ancestor sector 51 and is LOW quality, so it may misstate this industry's compensation-to-receipts structure.
a.change_evidence: A representative task-level time-use study for software-publisher employees, split by occupation and firm size and measuring realized rather than theoretical AI time savings, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: The industry is unusually concentrated in digital knowledge work: BLS reports 45.59% of employment in computer and mathematical occupations, 17.03% in management, 13.65% in business and financial operations, 10.45% in sales, and 7.88% in office and administrative support. Generative AI can materially assist coding, testing, documentation, product analysis, marketing, customer support, and back-office work, while production reliability, security, architecture, customer context, and legal responsibility constrain full task substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS's projected 15% ten-year employment growth for software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers is converted to an approximately 7% five-year midpoint proxy, then widened for software-publisher service demand. Occupational employment growth is not constant-price industry demand: it may understate output growth when productivity rises and overstate publisher demand because most developers work outside software publishers.
caveats: Employment growth is only a proxy for constant-price demand and spans occupations across many industries.
Product mix varies from mature maintenance software to rapidly growing AI-native applications.
Nominal subscription growth can reflect price changes rather than real service demand.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price five-year forecast for U.S. NAICS 513210 output or customer software spending, with explicit treatment of AI-related substitution and price changes, would materially change this multiplier.
d5.rationale: Demand benefits from continued digitization, cybersecurity investment, AI, IoT, robotics, and software embedded in more products. BLS projects 15% growth for developer, QA, and tester employment from 2024 to 2034 and specifically attributes strong demand to those applications. The five-year range remains wide because generative AI can enable internal builds, compress per-seat usage, create rapid substitutes, and redistribute value toward infrastructure providers.

caveats: The NAICS definition establishes activity, not profitability, independence, size, or revenue recurrence.
The pricing survey covers 100 SaaS companies and is not a representative census of all software publishers.
The firm-count input n is a declared dataset gap and must remain MISSING; this estimate does not replace or infer n.
e.change_evidence: A complete establishment-level census linking NAICS 513210 firms to independent ownership, recurring revenue, positive EBITDA, and lower-middle-market size would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: A majority of economically meaningful software publishers likely fit the external-customer, recurring-product, positive-EBITDA lens, especially established B2B subscription and maintenance businesses. The eligible share is reduced by consumer and game publishers, advertising-supported products, perpetual-license volatility, very small owner-dependent products, venture-backed firms prioritizing growth over EBITDA, and products embedded in broader companies.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 51 ($506,769,522,547) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 51 ($2,139,449,301,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 51. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3593 (margins.json[513]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Software (System & Application) EBITDA/Sales 35.93% (NAICS 2022 513 = publishing incl. software)); revenue/employee $588,729 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 51); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Persistence differs sharply between system-of-record software and lightly differentiated point applications.
The estimate concerns the need for an operating entity, not whether current staffing levels persist.
AI may create new publisher categories while displacing existing products.
o.change_evidence: Cohort evidence on publisher displacement, customer insourcing, product consolidation, and renewal behavior after adoption of generative-AI development tools would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Customers generally still need an accountable publisher to operate, secure, update, integrate, support, and contractually stand behind production software. Installed workflows, proprietary data, integrations, compliance, and switching costs reinforce operator persistence. AI-assisted internal development, open-source components, low-code tools, platform bundling, and rapidly replicable point solutions can nevertheless eliminate or consolidate some standalone publisher roles.

caveats: Pricing architecture indicates potential value capture but does not directly measure retained labor savings.
The survey is small and focused on SaaS, excluding important software-publisher business models.
AI may simultaneously lower costs and increase competitive entry or infrastructure spending.
q.change_evidence: Publisher-level evidence comparing AI-driven unit-cost savings with subsequent prices, gross margins, churn, seat counts, and cloud costs across comparable products would materially change the retention estimate.
q.rationale: Recurring subscriptions, high gross margins, switching costs, product differentiation, and usage-linked pricing allow many publishers to retain a meaningful share of AI-enabled cost savings. Metronome's SaaS survey indicates widespread usage-based pricing, which can align price with customer value. Retention is limited by intense competition, customer procurement pressure, falling development barriers, open-source and AI-native substitutes, seat compression, and incremental cloud or inference expense.

rho.bridge: Current AI-use rates for the broad Information sector and AI adoption among global technology professionals are translated into the five-year fraction of addressable software-publisher tasks actually realized. The bridge raises realization above today's firm-use rate because the horizon is five years, but discounts for integration, trust, security, review, and change-management frictions. Broad-sector and individual-professional evidence may overstate realization at smaller publishers with weaker data and engineering systems.
caveats: BTOS covers the entire Information sector rather than NAICS 513210 alone.
Reported tool use and perceived productivity are not equivalent to durable labor-cost realization.
DORA's trust findings imply review work can offset some gross time savings.
rho.change_evidence: Longitudinal U.S. software-publisher evidence linking deployed AI tools to audited labor hours or cost per release, segmented by firm size, would materially change the realization estimate.
rho.rationale: Census BTOS reported 39.7% current AI use in the Information sector as of May 3, 2026, with about 42% expected use, while Google DORA found high developer adoption and reported productivity benefits alongside low trust. Software publishers have strong technical capacity and direct incentives to incorporate AI, but production controls, customer data restrictions, model reliability, and organizational redesign will prevent all addressable tasks from realizing savings within five years.

caveats: Kroll's figures are global and cover the broader software sector across transaction sizes.
Deal counts do not identify unique sellers or provide the denominator needed for a seller-incidence rate.
Financing conditions and software valuation cycles can make a single period unrepresentative of a full five-year window.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year panel matching U.S. NAICS 513210 lower-middle-market firms to completed control sales, with a stable eligible-firm denominator and exclusion of minority financings, would materially change this range.
s5.rationale: Software is an active acquisition category, and Kroll reported more than 700 global software transactions in each of three consecutive quarters in 2025, with year-to-date volume already matching full-year 2024. That supports meaningful five-year seller incidence, but the absence of a U.S. lower-middle-market firm denominator and the inclusion of strategic, venture, growth, and large-cap transactions require a wide, conservative range.

principal_driver: A large share of high-cost digital work is addressable by AI inside a recurring-revenue product model with strong technical capacity to deploy it.
principal_weakness: The lower-middle-market firm count is missing, while AI simultaneously lowers product-development barriers and may compress seats and pricing, making aggregate availability and retained value unusually uncertain.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily engaged in software publishing and distinguishes the activity from custom software services.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 513210 Software Publishers

sources.supports: Provides employment and wage distribution across computer, management, business, sales, and administrative occupations used to bridge to AI-addressable task share.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Software Publishers

sources.supports: Reports current and expected AI use in the Information sector and adoption differences by firm size.
sources.title: Large Firms With At Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users

sources.supports: Reports developer AI adoption, perceived productivity and quality benefits, and low trust that constrains realization.
sources.title: How Are Developers Using AI? Inside Google's 2025 DORA Report

sources.supports: Provides 2024-2034 employment projections and demand drivers including AI, IoT, robotics, cybersecurity, and software embedded in more products.
sources.title: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers

sources.supports: Documents strong recent global software transaction volume and value, used cautiously to inform seller incidence.
sources.title: Global Software Sector Update—Fall 2025

sources.supports: Describes adoption of usage-based pricing among surveyed SaaS companies and its alignment of revenue with customer usage and value.
sources.title: State of Usage-Based Pricing 2025

title: Software Publishers
